event: Beothuk and other Indigenous peoples of nearby Newfoundland are known to have ranged through the surrounding waters and islands, though the archipelago itself was used only seasonally.
Portuguese navigator João Álvares Fagundes sights the islands and names them the 'Eleven Thousand Virgins' on the feast day of Saint Ursula.
French explorer Jacques Cartier records the islands during his Atlantic voyages, noting their use by Breton and Norman fishermen.
French fishermen from Brittany, Normandy, and the Basque Country establish seasonal cod-fishing stations on the islands.
France formally takes possession of Saint Pierre and Miquelon, establishing a small permanent settlement focused on the cod fishery.
English forces attack and destroy the French settlement during conflicts over North Atlantic fishing grounds.
The Treaty of Utrecht cedes the islands to Britain along with French claims in Newfoundland, ending French control.
The Treaty of Paris returns Saint Pierre and Miquelon to France as a base for its North Atlantic fishery after the Seven Years' War.
British forces capture the islands, deport the French population, and destroy the settlements during the American Revolutionary War.
The Treaty of Paris restores the islands to France, allowing returning settlers to rebuild the fishing communities.
Britain again seizes the islands and deports the inhabitants amid the French Revolutionary Wars.
The Treaty of Amiens returns the islands to France, though renewed war soon brought further disruption.
British forces reoccupy the islands and deport the French settlers as the Napoleonic Wars resume.
The Treaty of Paris definitively restores Saint Pierre and Miquelon to France after Napoleon's defeat.
French settlers, many from Basque, Breton, and Norman fishing families, repopulate the islands, beginning continuous French settlement.
The islands flourish as a major center of the North Atlantic cod fishery, attracting fishing fleets and supporting a growing population.
The Pointe aux Canons lighthouse is established to guide vessels into Saint-Pierre harbour.
Saint Pierre and Miquelon is administered as a French colony with its own colonial governor and council.
A devastating tidal surge and storms damage the isthmus linking the two parts of Miquelon, reshaping local settlement patterns.
The cod fishery dominates the economy, but competition and overfishing begin to strain the islands' traditional livelihood.
The onset of Prohibition in the United States transforms the islands into a major transshipment hub for smuggled alcohol.
At the height of the bootlegging boom, vast quantities of liquor pass through Saint-Pierre's warehouses for smuggling into North America.
The repeal of American Prohibition collapses the lucrative liquor-trade economy, plunging the islands into economic depression.
Free French and Gaullist forces under Admiral Émile Muselier seize the islands from the Vichy administration on Christmas Eve, sparking a diplomatic crisis with the Allies.
The islands remain under Free French and then republican French control as the Second World War ends.
Voters in Saint Pierre and Miquelon approve the new French constitution, retaining their status as a French overseas territory.
Saint Pierre and Miquelon becomes a French overseas department (département d'outre-mer), tightening its integration with metropolitan France.
The islands' status is changed to a territorial collectivity with special tax and customs arrangements outside the European Community.
An international arbitration tribunal sets a maritime boundary granting France a limited exclusive economic zone, far smaller than claimed, in its dispute with Canada.
Canada's moratorium on northern cod fishing devastates the islands' economy, ending centuries of reliance on the cod fishery.
A French constitutional reform reclassifies the islands as an overseas collectivity (collectivité d'outre-mer).
Residents of Saint Pierre and Miquelon vote in French presidential and legislative elections as French citizens within an overseas collectivity.
Offshore oil and gas exploration prospects and tourism are promoted as the islands seek to diversify their fishing-dependent economy.
The COVID-19 pandemic prompts strict travel and ferry restrictions, isolating the small archipelago to limit infection.
Saint Pierre and Miquelon continues as France's last remaining North American territory, sustaining its distinct French-Atlantic identity and culture.
